conditions.oneSentence: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is a progressive lung disease marked by persistent airflow limitation and respiratory symptoms.
conditions.keyMechanism: Airway and alveolar injury reduce expiratory airflow reserve, so breathing becomes mechanically harder during exertion and flares.
conditions.timeScale: usually evolves over years with episodic exacerbations
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe chronic cough, sputum, wheeze, or breathlessness that slowly narrows daily activity.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see why obstructed exhalation causes dyspnea and air trapping.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: chronic irritant exposure injures airways and alveoli
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: reduced airflow reserve creates exertional dyspnea and exacerbation vulnerability
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Narrowed small airways and damaged elastic recoil make exhalation incomplete.

labs.name: pulse oximetry
labs.expectedFinding: may be low in advanced disease or exacerbation

imaging.name: chest imaging when clinically indicated
imaging.expectedFinding: may show hyperinflation or alternate pathology

diagnosticLogic: Confirm airflow obstruction with spirometry when feasible.
Treat symptoms and exacerbation risk without relying on symptoms alone.

differentials.whyConsidered: can also cause wheeze and obstruction
differentials.whyLessLikely: COPD is more tied to chronic exposure and persistent limitation

firstLine: inhaled bronchodilator therapy guided by symptoms
escalation: vaccination and pulmonary rehabilitation when indicated
notes: Do not imply a single inhaler choice fits all COPD patients.

conditions.mechanismClaim: COPD produces persistent airflow limitation with chronic respiratory symptoms such as cough, sputum, wheeze, and dyspnea.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Spirometry is the key objective test used to confirm airflow obstruction in suspected COPD.
conditions.managementClaim: COPD management includes smoking cessation, symptom-directed inhaled therapy, vaccination, rehabilitation when indicated, and exacerbation prevention.
conditions.safetyClaim: Sudden worsening dyspnea, hypoxemia, or infection clues require escalation rather than routine chronic-care framing.
conditions.teachingPoint: The mystery should show shrinking airflow reserve before naming the disease.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Stable COPD education must not blur into unsafe management of severe exacerbations.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A narrowed felt airway tunnel where exhaled air backs up like traffic behind a collapsed exit.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A case-board gauge warning that oxygenation and work of breathing can convert chronic clues into urgent action.

conditions.oneSentence: Community-acquired pneumonia is an acute infection of lung parenchyma acquired outside the hospital setting.
conditions.keyMechanism: Infectious invasion of distal airspaces triggers inflammation and consolidation that impair gas exchange.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often feel feverish and short of breath with cough, pleuritic discomfort, or fatigue.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story needs to show why fluid-filled airspaces cause breathlessness and hypoxemia.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: microbes reach distal airspaces and trigger inflammation
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Inflammatory exudate replaces air in affected alveoli and creates systemic illness clues.

historyClues: acute respiratory illness in the community
physicalExamClues: focal crackles or bronchial breath sounds

imaging.name: chest radiograph
imaging.expectedFinding: new pulmonary infiltrate in compatible illness

diagnosticLogic: Combine compatible symptoms with imaging and severity assessment.
Site-of-care and empiric therapy depend on severity and risk factors.

differentials.whyLessLikely: focal infiltrate and systemic severity support pneumonia

firstLine: supportive oxygen and fluids when needed
escalation: hospital-level care for severe disease or unstable vitals
notes: Avoid teaching antibiotics as automatic for every cough.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Community-acquired pneumonia infects lung parenchyma and can produce inflammatory consolidation that impairs gas exchange.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Adult CAP guidance uses clinical features, imaging evidence of pneumonia, and severity assessment to guide diagnosis and site of care.
conditions.managementClaim: CAP treatment uses severity- and risk-guided empiric antimicrobial therapy plus supportive care.
conditions.safetyClaim: Sepsis, hypoxemia, or severe respiratory distress should trigger urgent escalation.
conditions.teachingPoint: Let learners see alveoli filling with inflammatory material before the diagnosis is named.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: The story should separate mild outpatient CAP from severe pneumonia needing urgent care.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A soft red-and-gold alveolar neighborhood where air bubbles are replaced by cloudy inflammatory mist.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: Two case-board paths showing outpatient clues versus oxygen/sepsis clues.

conditions.oneSentence: Urinary tract infection is infection within the urinary tract, commonly presenting in primary care as acute cystitis when limited to the bladder.
conditions.keyMechanism: Bacteria can ascend into the urinary tract, inflaming the bladder mucosa and causing urgency, dysuria, and frequency.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients commonly report burning urination, urgency, frequency, or suprapubic discomfort.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to distinguish bladder-limited symptoms from upper-tract warning signs.

conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: irritated bladder signaling creates dysuria, urgency, and frequency
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Mucosal inflammation lowers the threshold for painful voiding and urgency signals.

physicalExamClues: absence of flank pain or systemic toxicity supports bladder-limited framing

labs.name: urinalysis
labs.expectedFinding: pyuria or nitrite/leukocyte esterase may support UTI

diagnosticLogic: Typical uncomplicated cystitis can be recognized clinically, while complicated or upper-tract features need broader evaluation.
Fever, flank pain, pregnancy, male sex, obstruction, or systemic illness changes the frame.

differentials.whyLessLikely: isolated urinary urgency/frequency without vaginal or STI clues supports cystitis

firstLine: risk-appropriate antimicrobial therapy when bacterial cystitis is likely
escalation: evaluate for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI when systemic or flank symptoms appear
notes: Do not represent every UTI as uncomplicated.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Lower UTI symptoms commonly reflect bladder mucosal inflammation from urinary pathogens.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Uncomplicated cystitis is distinguished from complicated or upper-tract infection by symptoms, risk factors, and red flags.
conditions.managementClaim: UTI treatment should match uncomplicated versus complicated disease context and support antimicrobial stewardship.
conditions.safetyClaim: Fever, flank pain, pregnancy, obstruction, or systemic illness should shift the story toward complicated infection review.
conditions.teachingPoint: The mystery should make location the key clue: bladder symptoms are different from kidney/systemic danger.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: The narrative should not flatten pyelonephritis or complicated UTI into routine cystitis.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A felt bladder-wall corridor glowing with tiny alarm lights as urine passes.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A ladder from bladder to kidney with warning placards for fever, flank pain, and systemic illness.

conditions.oneSentence: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is troublesome reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus with symptoms or complications.
conditions.keyMechanism: Refluxate escapes the stomach barrier and irritates esophageal mucosa, creating heartburn, regurgitation, or complications.
conditions.timeScale: recurrent or chronic with meal- and position-related flares
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe burning retrosternal discomfort, sour regurgitation, or symptoms after meals or lying down.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story needs a visual valve/border that explains reflux without overpromising one cause.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: gastric reflux crosses the esophagogastric barrier
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: esophageal irritation produces heartburn and regurgitation clues
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Acid and reflux contents stimulate sensitive esophageal tissue and can cause mucosal injury.

commonSymptoms: chest discomfort after cardiac causes considered

imaging.name: upper endoscopy when alarm or refractory features exist
imaging.purpose: evaluate mucosal disease or alternate diagnoses

diagnosticLogic: Classic symptoms can justify empiric therapy in appropriate patients.
Alarm symptoms or refractory symptoms require objective evaluation.

differentials.whyLessLikely: reflux pattern may fit after urgent cardiac risk is considered

escalation: endoscopy or reflux testing for alarm, refractory, or pre-procedural cases
notes: Chest pain framing must explicitly respect cardiac evaluation.

conditions.mechanismClaim: GERD involves reflux of gastric contents that causes troublesome symptoms or complications.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Classic GERD symptoms may support empiric therapy, while alarm or refractory symptoms prompt endoscopic or objective evaluation.
conditions.managementClaim: GERD management includes lifestyle measures, acid suppression when appropriate, and further testing for selected cases.
conditions.safetyClaim: Chest pain, dysphagia, bleeding, weight loss, or refractory symptoms require careful escalation rather than routine reflux framing.
conditions.teachingPoint: Show reflux as a barrier failure and irritation story, not merely too much acid.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: The guide must keep alarm symptoms and cardiac chest pain boundaries explicit.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A velvet valve at the stomach border with warm amber reflux mist sneaking upward.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: Case-board placards for dysphagia, bleeding, weight loss, and chest pain risk.

conditions.oneSentence: Hyperlipidemia is an abnormal lipid profile that can increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk over time.
conditions.keyMechanism: Atherogenic lipoproteins enter arterial walls, fuel plaque biology, and raise long-term risk of myocardial infarction and stroke.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Most patients feel no symptoms, so the story turns a quiet lab clue into a future-risk mystery.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to understand why an invisible blood test can matter clinically.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: atherogenic particles accumulate in the arterial wall
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: plaque risk builds silently before clinical events
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Long-term lipid exposure contributes to atherosclerotic plaque formation and instability risk.

historyClues: family history of premature cardiovascular disease
physicalExamClues: xanthomas may appear in severe inherited disorders

labs.name: lipid panel
labs.purpose: measure LDL-C and related lipid values
labs.expectedFinding: elevated atherogenic lipid measures may guide risk management

imaging.name: coronary artery calcium in selected cases
imaging.purpose: risk reclassification when decision uncertainty remains
imaging.expectedFinding: higher burden can support intensifying prevention

diagnosticLogic: Use lipid values together with ASCVD risk, diabetes, family history, and risk enhancers.
Do not teach LDL as a symptom-producing toxin.

differentials.whyConsidered: very high LDL or family history changes urgency
differentials.whyLessLikely: moderate dyslipidemia may reflect multifactorial risk

escalation: intensify therapy for high-risk patients or inadequate response
notes: Use risk-based framing, not universal identical treatment.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Atherogenic lipoproteins contribute to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk over time.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Lipid management decisions use lipid values together with ASCVD risk estimation and risk-enhancing factors.
conditions.managementClaim: Management combines lifestyle intervention and risk-guided lipid-lowering therapy, commonly including statins when indicated.
conditions.safetyClaim: Very high LDL-C, diabetes, established ASCVD, or strong family history can change treatment urgency and targets.
conditions.teachingTitle: Silent risk can still be dangerous
conditions.teachingPoint: The mystery should convert a quiet lab abnormality into visible artery-wall stakes.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Avoid one-size-fits-all statin storytelling; risk categories matter.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: Tiny felt lipid couriers delivering golden cargo into an artery-wall construction site.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A detective board connecting LDL, diabetes, family history, CAC, and prevention intensity.

conditions.oneSentence: Major depressive disorder is a mood disorder with persistent depressive symptoms and functional impairment.
conditions.keyMechanism: Mood, cognition, sleep, appetite, energy, and reward systems shift together, creating a syndrome rather than ordinary sadness alone.
conditions.timeScale: symptoms persist over weeks or longer
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may describe low mood, loss of interest, sleep or appetite changes, fatigue, guilt, poor concentration, or suicidal thoughts.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need a respectful frame that depression is disabling, not a character flaw.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: mood and reward regulation become persistently disrupted
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: symptoms impair daily function and safety may be at risk
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Affective, cognitive, neurovegetative, and behavioral symptoms reinforce one another.

physicalExamClues: rule out medical contributors when indicated

labs.name: targeted labs when clinically indicated
labs.purpose: evaluate medical mimics such as thyroid disease or anemia

diagnosticLogic: Diagnose through symptom pattern, duration, impairment, exclusions, and safety assessment.
Screening tools support but do not replace clinical assessment.

differentials.whyConsidered: depression can occur in bipolar illness
differentials.whyLessLikely: absence of manic or hypomanic history supports unipolar framing

escalation: urgent safety care for suicidal intent or severe impairment
notes: Keep the tone humane and non-stigmatizing.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Major depression involves persistent mood and interest changes with cognitive, sleep, appetite, energy, and functional symptoms.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Diagnosis relies on clinical symptom pattern, duration, impairment, exclusion of alternate causes, and safety assessment.
conditions.managementClaim: Treatment commonly uses psychotherapy, medication, or both, tailored to severity, preferences, comorbidities, and safety.
conditions.safetyClaim: Suicidal ideation, psychosis, mania history, or inability to function safely requires urgent escalation.
conditions.teachingPoint: The mystery should link mood, reward, sleep, and function without reducing the illness to sadness.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Every MDD story must treat self-harm risk as clinically material, not optional drama.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A warm, tactile control room with reward lights dimmed and sleep-energy levers out of sync.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A respectful emergency beacon on the case board for suicidality and severe impairment.
conditions.secondarySourceLabel: NCBI Bookshelf major depressive disorder review

conditions.oneSentence: Generalized anxiety disorder is persistent, excessive worry with psychological and physical symptoms that impair function.
conditions.keyMechanism: Threat-monitoring and worry loops become overactive, keeping body and attention systems on alert even when danger is not immediate.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may feel unable to control worry and may have restlessness, muscle tension, poor sleep, fatigue, or concentration problems.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story needs to show anxiety as a body-mind alarm loop, not weakness.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: worry and threat-monitoring loops become persistent
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: autonomic and muscle-tension symptoms impair sleep and function
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Cognitive worry and physical arousal reinforce each other over time.

physicalExamClues: screen for medical or substance contributors when indicated

labs.name: targeted medical evaluation
labs.purpose: exclude mimics when symptoms suggest another cause

diagnosticLogic: Assess worry pattern, duration, control, somatic symptoms, impairment, and alternate causes.
Panic disorder, depression, thyroid disease, and substance effects can overlap.

differentials.whyLessLikely: GAD centers on persistent worry rather than discrete panic attacks alone

firstLine: education and active monitoring for mild symptoms
CBT or other evidence-based psychological therapy
SSRI or SNRI when medication is appropriate
escalation: specialist referral for severe impairment, risk, or poor response
notes: Avoid portraying anxiety as solved by simple reassurance.

conditions.mechanismClaim: GAD is characterized by persistent excessive worry with psychological and physical symptoms that affect function.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: GAD assessment requires pattern, duration, impairment, and evaluation for overlapping or alternative causes.
conditions.managementClaim: Guideline-based GAD care uses stepped care, psychological therapies, and medication options such as SSRIs or SNRIs when appropriate.
conditions.safetyClaim: Severe impairment, self-harm risk, substance misuse, or diagnostic uncertainty should trigger escalation.
conditions.teachingPoint: The story should visualize chronic false-alarm physiology with compassion.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Show treatment as skills, monitoring, and tailored medication rather than one magic switch.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A felt control console where tiny alarm bells keep ringing across unrelated worry channels.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A staircase showing education, therapy, medication, and specialist escalation.

aliases: osteoarthritis of hand hip or knee
conditions.oneSentence: Osteoarthritis is a whole-joint disorder involving cartilage, bone, synovium, pain, and function changes.
conditions.keyMechanism: Mechanical, inflammatory, and structural joint changes alter the joint environment and produce pain and stiffness.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often report activity-related joint pain, brief stiffness, and progressive limits in daily movement.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see the joint as a whole system, not just worn cartilage.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: joint tissues remodel under mechanical and inflammatory stress
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: pain and stiffness reduce function even when imaging severity varies
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Cartilage, bone, synovium, and pain pathways interact to shape symptoms.

historyClues: age, prior injury, obesity, repetitive load

imaging.name: plain radiographs when needed
imaging.purpose: support diagnosis or evaluate severity/alternate diagnoses
imaging.expectedFinding: joint-space narrowing or osteophytes may be present

diagnosticLogic: Clinical pattern often drives diagnosis; imaging should fit the patient story.
Inflammatory arthritis, fracture, infection, or referred pain change the workup.

differentials.whyConsidered: can cause joint pain and stiffness
differentials.whyLessLikely: OA stiffness is typically shorter and pattern is more mechanical

escalation: topical or oral NSAIDs when appropriate
injections or surgical evaluation for selected severe disease
notes: Do not equate x-ray severity with the whole patient experience.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Osteoarthritis involves structural and symptomatic changes across the whole joint, not cartilage loss alone.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: OA diagnosis is grounded in clinical pattern, with imaging used selectively to support diagnosis or evaluate alternatives.
conditions.managementClaim: Guideline-based OA care emphasizes exercise, education, weight management when relevant, and risk-aware analgesic choices.
conditions.safetyClaim: Hot swollen joints, trauma, systemic illness, or rapidly progressive symptoms require alternate-diagnosis review.
conditions.teachingPoint: Make the joint environment and function loss the mystery, not only cartilage erosion.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: NSAID and procedure decisions must be individualized rather than automatic.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A felt joint workshop where cartilage cushions, bone edges, and synovial curtains all show wear and irritation.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A red lamp distinguishing routine OA stiffness from infection, crystal arthritis, or trauma clues.

conditions.canonicalDiseaseName: Low back pain with red-flag screening
conditions.oneSentence: Low back pain is common and usually nonspecific, but red-flag screening identifies patients needing urgent evaluation.
conditions.keyMechanism: Most low back pain reflects mechanical or nonspecific pain pathways, while a small subset signals fracture, infection, malignancy, or neurologic compromise.
conditions.timeScale: acute to chronic depending on episode
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often report pain with movement or posture, while clinicians look for danger clues that change the pathway.

physiologyPrerequisites.topic: spine anatomy and neurologic warning signs
physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to separate common pain from rare but serious causes.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: nonspecific mechanical pain starts in spinal tissues or pain-processing pathways
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: red flags redirect the case toward serious pathology evaluation
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Neurologic deficits, infection clues, cancer history, trauma, or systemic symptoms change probability and urgency.

presentation.typicalTimecourse: often improves, but chronic and recurrent courses occur

labs.name: targeted inflammatory labs
labs.purpose: evaluate suspected infection or inflammatory disease
labs.expectedFinding: not needed for uncomplicated nonspecific pain

imaging.name: selective spine imaging
imaging.purpose: evaluate red flags or persistent/progressive concerning features
imaging.expectedFinding: not routine for uncomplicated acute nonspecific pain

diagnosticLogic: Screen for red flags first.
Avoid routine imaging when uncomplicated nonspecific pain has no concerning features.

differentials.whyConsidered: bowel/bladder dysfunction or saddle anesthesia is urgent
differentials.whyLessLikely: absence of neurologic red flags supports routine pathway

firstLine: education and reassurance when no red flags
escalation: urgent imaging or referral for serious red flags
notes: Red flags are the gate; they must be shown before routine back-pain advice.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Most low back pain in primary care is nonspecific, but screening must look for serious causes.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Red flags such as progressive neurologic deficits, infection risk, cancer clues, trauma, or cauda equina symptoms change the evaluation pathway.
conditions.managementClaim: ACP guidance emphasizes noninvasive, often nonpharmacologic care for uncomplicated low back pain.
conditions.safetyClaim: Bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, progressive weakness, fever with risk factors, cancer history, or major trauma requires urgent escalation.
conditions.teachingTitle: First sort danger from common pain
conditions.teachingPoint: The case should make screening the detective move before any treatment advice.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Routine reassurance is only safe after serious-pathology clues are actively checked.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A soft spine tower with ordinary creaks below and bright red neurologic warning beacons above.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: Detectives pass through a checkpoint listing cancer, infection, fracture, and cauda equina clues.
conditions.secondarySourceLabel: NCBI Bookshelf low back pain review

conditions.oneSentence: Allergic rhinitis is an allergic inflammatory condition of the nasal mucosa causing congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, or itching.
conditions.keyMechanism: Allergen exposure triggers IgE-mediated immune signaling in nasal tissue, producing itch, mucus, congestion, and sneezing.
conditions.timeScale: seasonal, perennial, or episodic with exposures
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe sneezing, itchy nose or eyes, runny nose, congestion, and sleep or school/work disruption.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see why harmless pollen can become a dramatic alarm.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: allergen exposure activates allergic nasal inflammation
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: mucus, itch, sneezing, and congestion disrupt breathing and sleep
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Mast-cell and inflammatory mediator signaling changes vascular leak, mucus, and nerve sensitivity.

commonSigns: pale swollen nasal mucosa may be present
physicalExamClues: allergic shiners or nasal crease may appear

labs.name: specific IgE testing when needed
labs.purpose: identify allergens when diagnosis is uncertain or targeted therapy is needed

diagnosticLogic: Clinical history and exam often identify AR.
Testing is useful when empiric treatment fails, diagnosis is uncertain, or allergen identification changes management.

differentials.whyConsidered: can cause runny nose and congestion
differentials.whyLessLikely: itching, sneezing, and exposure pattern favor allergy

firstLine: intranasal corticosteroid or antihistamine strategy based on symptoms
escalation: allergy testing or immunotherapy referral in selected cases
notes: Avoid implying allergy testing is required for every classic case.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Allergic rhinitis symptoms arise from allergic inflammation of the nasal mucosa after relevant allergen exposure.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: A clinical diagnosis is appropriate when history and exam fit allergic rhinitis; IgE testing is reserved for uncertainty, poor response, or targeted allergen planning.
conditions.managementClaim: Management combines allergen control, intranasal or oral therapies, and selected immunotherapy referral.
conditions.safetyClaim: Poor control, asthma symptoms, recurrent sinus disease, or diagnostic uncertainty should prompt broader review.
conditions.teachingTitle: The nose as an alarm gate
conditions.teachingPoint: Show pollen as a false intruder that starts a real mucosal response.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Allergic rhinitis stories should check asthma and sleep impact rather than treating the nose in isolation.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A velvet nasal hallway where pollen specks trigger overenthusiastic alarm bells and mucus sprinklers.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A map connecting nose, eyes, sinuses, sleep, and lower airway clues.

conditions.oneSentence: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic inflammatory skin disease with itch, eczematous lesions, and barrier dysfunction.
conditions.keyMechanism: Skin barrier weakness and immune inflammation reinforce itch, scratching, dryness, and flare cycles.
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often experience intense itch, dry inflamed patches, sleep disruption, and flare triggers.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see why scratching feels urgent but worsens the barrier.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: skin barrier dysfunction and immune inflammation reinforce each other
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: itch-scratch cycles worsen lesions and sleep
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Barrier loss permits irritation while inflammation sensitizes itch pathways.

diagnosticLogic: Diagnosis is usually clinical based on itch, morphology, distribution, chronicity, and exclusions.
Infection or severe refractory disease changes management.

differentials.whyLessLikely: chronic atopic pattern and distribution support AD

escalation: phototherapy or systemic therapy for appropriate moderate-to-severe disease
notes: Systemic corticosteroids should not be casually depicted as routine long-term care.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Atopic dermatitis involves skin barrier dysfunction and immune inflammation that drive itch and eczematous lesions.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Atopic dermatitis is usually diagnosed clinically, while mimics and infection require targeted evaluation.
conditions.managementClaim: Management includes barrier repair, topical anti-inflammatory therapy, trigger control, and selected advanced therapy for moderate-to-severe disease.
conditions.safetyClaim: Infected skin, severe sleep loss, widespread disease, or systemic-therapy decisions require careful escalation.
conditions.teachingTitle: The barrier breaks, the itch grows
conditions.teachingPoint: Show barrier repair as the core detective solution, not just rash suppression.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: Advanced treatments and infection clues must stay separate from routine moisturizer education.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A soft brick wall of skin cells with gaps, dry fibers, and tiny itch sparks escaping.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A warning flare beside crusting, oozing, sleep-loss, and widespread-disease clues.
conditions.primarySourceLabel: American Academy of Dermatology atopic dermatitis guidelines

conditions.oneSentence: Obesity and metabolic syndrome describe excess or dysfunctional adiposity with cardiometabolic risk and complications.
conditions.keyMechanism: Adipose tissue, insulin resistance, inflammation, blood pressure, lipids, and glucose regulation interact to raise long-term risk.
conditions.timeScale: chronic over years with modifiable trajectories
conditions.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may have no symptoms but face weight stigma, metabolic risk, sleep apnea, joint pain, fatty liver risk, or diabetes risk.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need to see adiposity as biology and risk, not moral failure.

conditions.pathophysiologyEvent: adiposity and insulin resistance alter metabolic signaling
conditions.clinicalConsequenceEvent: blood pressure, lipids, glucose, liver, sleep, and joint risks cluster
conditions.clinicalConsequenceMechanism: Metabolic and mechanical complications interact and require individualized goals.

commonSymptoms: fatigue or exertional limitation may occur
physicalExamClues: waist circumference and blood pressure context

labs.name: A1C, fasting glucose, lipid panel, liver tests when indicated
labs.expectedFinding: may show dysglycemia, dyslipidemia, or other complications

diagnosticLogic: Assess adiposity plus complications and function, not BMI alone.
Screen for cardiometabolic, sleep, liver, mood, and joint comorbidities.

differentials.whyLessLikely: multifactorial adiposity with metabolic complications is more common

firstLine: nutrition, physical activity, sleep, and behavioral support
escalation: anti-obesity medication or bariatric surgery when criteria and goals fit
notes: Avoid shame-based imagery or implying willpower is the mechanism.

conditions.mechanismClaim: Obesity and metabolic syndrome involve adiposity-linked metabolic risk, including insulin resistance, blood pressure, lipid, and glucose abnormalities.
conditions.diagnosticClaim: Assessment should evaluate adiposity-related complications and cardiometabolic risk rather than BMI alone.
conditions.managementClaim: Care combines lifestyle, behavioral, pharmacologic, and procedural options matched to disease stage, complications, and patient goals.
conditions.safetyClaim: Weight-centered care must remain stigma-free and screen for comorbid conditions and medication or endocrine contributors.
conditions.teachingPoint: The mystery should teach metabolic signaling and complications without moralizing body size.
conditions.safetyTeachingPoint: The art and wording must avoid shame, caricature, or blame.
conditions.visualAnchorDescription: A warm felt network linking adipose tissue, liver, pancreas, blood vessels, sleep, and joints.
conditions.safetyVisualDescription: A reviewer badge reminding the detectives to separate biology, risk, and dignity.
